# Electrical Plugs & Sockets in Rwanda: Types C, E, and G

> A guide to electrical plug and socket types used in Rwanda. Rwanda uses Types C, E, and G at 230V/50Hz. Includes the Type J database error correction.

Rwanda uses plug types C, E, and G at 230V / 50Hz.

## Plug Types

- Type C (Europlug): Compact, ungrounded two-pin plug, 4.0 mm diameter pins. Used for low-power devices like chargers, laptops, and small appliances. Fits directly into Type E sockets found throughout Rwanda. Dedicated Type C sockets are very rare.
- Type E (French/Belgian): Grounded plug where the socket provides the grounding pin, 4.8 mm diameter pins. Once Rwanda's standard, still the most widespread grounded socket in older buildings. Some homes built in the late 2010s still use Type E.
- Type F (Schuko): Two round pins with grounding clips on socket sides. Listed by the IEC for Rwanda but uncommon in practice — mostly encountered through imported European appliances.
- Type G (British BS 1363): Three rectangular pins in triangular pattern, with built-in fuse at 13 A. Rwanda's official standard, adopted as part of East African Community harmonisation. Required in all new construction since the early 2010s.

## Key Facts

- Voltage: 230 V
- Frequency: 50 Hz
- Compatible with most of Europe and Africa
- Type G is Rwanda's new standard, adopted as part of EAC harmonisation with Kenya, Tanzania, and Uganda

## Type J Database Error

Some international databases, notably those derived from IEC data, incorrectly listed Rwanda as using plug types C and J. Type J is the Swiss plug, used exclusively in Switzerland and Liechtenstein. This was a data entry error that propagated across reference databases. The IEC corrected Rwanda's listing in 2025 to types C, E, F, and G, removing the erroneous Type J.

## Travel Advice

- European two-pin (Type C) plugs work directly in Type E sockets, which are common in older buildings
- French/Belgian (Type E) plugs work in older buildings
- British (Type G) sockets are found in newer buildings and hotels, but UK travellers should carry an adapter in case their building only has Type E sockets
- US, Canadian, Chinese, Japanese, Australian, Indian and other non-European plug types will need an adapter
- A universal travel adapter is recommended for best coverage

## Sources

### Corrected sources

- Rwanda Standards Board — DEAS 495-2 specification for Type G (EAS 495) socket-outlets (rsb.gov.rw)
- Rwanda Standards Board — Public Review Notice #41 listing EAS 495-1 through 495-4 (rsb.gov.rw)
- IEC World Plugs (iec.ch/world-plugs) — lists C, E, F, G (corrected 2025, removing Type J)
- worldstandards.eu — lists C, E, F, G
- Wikipedia: Energy in Rwanda — lists C, E, G
- Wikivoyage: Rwanda — lists C, E, G
- Wise (wise.com/gb/plug-types/rwanda) — lists C, E, F, G
- Power Plugs & Sockets World (powerplugsockets.world/country/rwanda) — lists C, E, F, G
- World Power Plugs (world-power-plugs.com/rwanda) — lists C, E, F, G

### Sources still showing incorrect data

- power-plugs-sockets.com — still lists C, J (incorrect)
- Electrical Safety First (electricalsafetyfirst.org.uk) — still lists C, J (incorrect)

- Website: https://plugs.rw
